tasks.prompt: [Warm-up] Create a PodDisruptionBudget (PDB) and observe how it restricts Pod eviction when you run kubectl drain. Create a Deployment, set PDB with minAvailable: 2, then on a single-node cluster try a drain and observe the resulting message.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Test Deployment (replicas: 3)
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: pdb-demo
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: pdb-demo
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: pdb-demo
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: nginx
EOF

# 2. Create the PDB
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: pdb-demo-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: ___     # minimum Pods to keep
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ___

# 3. Check PDB status
kubectl get pdb
kubectl describe pdb pdb-demo-pdb

# 4. Get the node name (minikube)
kubectl get nodes

# 5. Attempt to drain the node (observe PDB violation)
kubectl drain ___ --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
tasks.solution:
# 2. Create the PDB
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: pdb-demo-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: pdb-demo
EOF

# 3. Check PDB status
kubectl get pdb
# NAME           MIN AVAILABLE   MAX UNAVAILABLE   ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS   AGE
# pdb-demo-pdb   2               N/A               1                     10s
# ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS=1: only 1 of the current 3 Pods may be evicted

kubectl describe pdb pdb-demo-pdb
# Status:
#   Disruptions Allowed:  1
#   Current Healthy:      3
#   Desired Healthy:      2
#   Expected Pods:        3

# 4. Drain node (minikube single-node example)
kubectl drain minikube --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
# evicting pod default/pdb-demo-xxxx
# error when evicting pods/"pdb-demo-xxxx": Cannot evict pod as it would violate
#   the pod's disruption budget. (cannot remove more than 1 on a single-node cluster)
# Or: 1 Pod is removed and the next eviction blocks

# 5. Restore the node with uncordon
kubectl uncordon minikube

# Clean up
kubectl delete deploy pdb-demo
kubectl delete pdb pdb-demo-pdb
tasks.explanation: A PodDisruptionBudget guarantees a minimum number of available Pods during voluntary disruptions such as drain operations and rolling updates. With minAvailable: 2, only 1 of the 3 Pods may be evicted at once. The ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS column reflects this. PDB does not apply to involuntary disruptions (node failures). During rolling updates, PDB also applies alongside maxUnavailable to ensure zero-downtime deployments.
hints: If the drain command blocks, press Ctrl+C to interrupt it and run kubectl uncordon <node-name> to restore the node.
You can use maxUnavailable instead of minAvailable. maxUnavailable: 1 means 'allow at most 1 Pod to be unavailable at a time'.
Run kubectl get pdb -o wide to see the ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS column and know in real time how many more Pods can be taken down.

tasks.prompt: [Core Practice] Install Argo Rollouts and create a Rollout object using the Canary deployment strategy. Define canary steps that progressively shift traffic from 20% to 50% to 100%, pausing at each stage for manual approval before proceeding.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Install Argo Rollouts
kubectl create namespace argo-rollouts
kubectl apply -n argo-rollouts -f https://github.com/argoproj/argo-rollouts/releases/latest/download/install.yaml

# Install the kubectl argo rollouts plugin (optional)
# https://argoproj.github.io/argo-rollouts/installation/

# 2. Rollout object (Canary strategy)
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Rollout
metadata:
  name: canary-demo
spec:
  replicas: 5
  strategy:
    canary:
      steps:
        - setWeight: ___    # Step 1: 20% traffic to canary
        - pause: {}         # wait for manual approval
        - setWeight: ___    # Step 2: 50%
        - pause:
            duration: ___   # auto-wait 30 seconds
        - setWeight: 100    # Step 3: 100% cutover complete
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: canary-demo
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: canary-demo
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: argoproj/rollouts-demo:blue    # v1
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080

---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: canary-demo-svc
spec:
  selector:
    app: canary-demo
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8080
tasks.solution:
# Create the Rollout
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Rollout
metadata:
  name: canary-demo
spec:
  replicas: 5
  strategy:
    canary:
      steps:
        - setWeight: 20
        - pause: {}
        - setWeight: 50
        - pause:
            duration: 30
        - setWeight: 100
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: canary-demo
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: canary-demo
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: argoproj/rollouts-demo:blue
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
EOF

# Check Rollout status
kubectl argo rollouts get rollout canary-demo --watch
# Or: kubectl get rollout canary-demo -n default

# Update to new version (start canary with yellow image)
kubectl argo rollouts set image canary-demo app=argoproj/rollouts-demo:yellow

# Status: 1 Pod (20%) is yellow, 4 Pods are blue, pausing at step 1
kubectl argo rollouts get rollout canary-demo
# NAME           KIND       STATUS        STEP  SET-WEIGHT  READY  DESIRED  UP-TO-DATE  AVAILABLE
# canary-demo    Rollout    ॥ Paused      1/5   20          5      5        1           5

# Manual approval (advance to next step)
kubectl argo rollouts promote canary-demo
# 50% step proceeds, then auto-waits 30 seconds

# Second promote
kubectl argo rollouts promote canary-demo
# 100% cutover complete
tasks.explanation: Argo Rollouts' Canary strategy executes the steps defined in the steps list in order. setWeight controls the percentage of traffic routed to canary Pods; with 5 replicas and setWeight: 20, 1 canary Pod is created (20%). pause: {} waits indefinitely until a promote command is issued. pause: {duration: 30} auto-advances after 30 seconds. This staged traffic shifting is the core of progressive delivery — at each step metrics can be reviewed and an abort command can roll back if issues are found.
hints: Without the kubectl argo rollouts plugin, check status with kubectl get rollout canary-demo -o yaml | grep -A20 'status:'
If a problem occurs during a canary deployment, kubectl argo rollouts abort canary-demo immediately rolls back to the stable version.
The exact percentage of setWeight is approximated by the replica count. With 5 replicas, setWeight: 20 gives 1 Pod (20%), setWeight: 40 gives 2 Pods (40%, rounded).

tasks.prompt: [Application] Combine a Rollout with a PDB to guarantee a minimum number of available Pods even during a Canary deployment. Create a PDB with minAvailable: 4 for a Rollout with 5 replicas. During a Canary deployment, attempt a kubectl drain to observe how the PDB constrains both the deployment and the drain simultaneously.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Create a PDB linked to the Rollout
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: canary-demo-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: ___    # keep 4 of 5 always available
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: canary-demo

# 2. Check PDB status after creation
kubectl get pdb canary-demo-pdb

# 3. Start a new version (begin canary deployment)
kubectl argo rollouts set image canary-demo app=argoproj/rollouts-demo:green

# 4. Attempt drain while canary is in progress (observe PDB constraint)
kubectl get nodes
kubectl drain ___ --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data --timeout=20s

# 5. Complete or abort the canary
kubectl argo rollouts abort canary-demo

# 6. Clean up
kubectl delete rollout canary-demo
kubectl delete pdb canary-demo-pdb
kubectl delete service canary-demo-svc
kubectl delete namespace argo-rollouts
tasks.solution:
# 1. Create the PDB
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: canary-demo-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: 4
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: canary-demo
EOF

# 2. PDB status
kubectl get pdb canary-demo-pdb
# NAME             MIN AVAILABLE   MAX UNAVAILABLE   ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS   AGE
# canary-demo-pdb  4               N/A               1                     5s

# 3. Start canary (green image)
kubectl argo rollouts set image canary-demo app=argoproj/rollouts-demo:green
# 1 of 5 is now green (canary), 4 are yellow (stable)

kubectl get pdb canary-demo-pdb
# ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS: 0  <-- during canary replacement the budget may reach 0

# 4. Attempt drain during canary (double constraint)
kubectl drain minikube --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data --timeout=20s
# error: unable to drain node "minikube" due to error:
#   Cannot evict pod as it would violate the pod's disruption budget

# 5. Roll back with abort
kubectl argo rollouts abort canary-demo
# Rollout is aborted, you should manually sync the Rollout or use the --force flag to override
kubectl argo rollouts undo canary-demo

# 6. Clean up
kubectl delete rollout canary-demo
kubectl delete pdb canary-demo-pdb
kubectl delete service canary-demo-svc
tasks.explanation: Using PDB together with a Canary deployment provides double protection for service availability. When both a canary Pod replacement (voluntary disruption) and a node drain (also voluntary) happen simultaneously, the PDB's ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS reaches 0 and both operations are blocked. In practice, schedule drain operations during a maintenance window, or set minAvailable low enough relative to replicas to allow both deployment and infrastructure maintenance to proceed concurrently.
hints: Having ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS at 0 blocking a drain is a situation that occurs frequently in production. You can use the --pod-selector flag to exclude specific Pods, or --disable-eviction (force delete), but neither is recommended.
kubectl argo rollouts undo reverts the rollout to the previous stable version. After an abort you must also run undo to fully restore the previous state.
PDB can use a percentage: minAvailable: '80%' keeps at least 80% of all Pods available at all times.
